title: How to Think
summary: A simple 3-step process for every move

steps.title: The Three Questions
steps.text:
Strong players don't just react — they follow a tiny mental routine on **every** move. The earlier you build the habit, the faster you'll improve.

## The three questions

1. **What is my opponent threatening?** Look at their last move. Did it attack a piece? Open a line? Prepare a tactic?
2. **What are MY checks, captures, and threats?** Scan the whole board. Tactics live in *forcing* moves. List them before you choose.
3. **What's the best reply if I play my candidate?** Imagine your move on the board, then ask: how would *I* respond as Black?

Three little questions. Asked honestly, they cut your blunder rate in half.

A quick example: imagine White just played {{Bg5}}, pinning a knight. Question 1: *what's threatened?* The knight can't move without exposing the queen. Question 2: *what are my forcing replies?* Maybe {{h6}} chases the bishop. Question 3: *what happens after h6?* The bishop retreats or trades.

> Three questions, one minute. The cheapest rating points you'll ever earn.

steps.title: The Blunder Check
steps.text:
Almost every lost game contains a moment where you played fast and missed something obvious. That moment is preventable.

## The 5-second blunder check

Before your hand leaves the piece, freeze and run a tiny scan:

- Is any of **my pieces** suddenly hanging (undefended, attacked)?
- Can my opponent give a **check** that wins something?
- Is there a **simple capture** for free that I just allowed?
- Did I just walk into a **fork, pin, or skewer**?

If the answer to all four is *no*, play the move. If *yes*, find another move.

This simple ritual costs nothing in classical games and only seconds in blitz. It catches most one-move blunders — the kind that haunt every chess player.

> Sit on your hands. Then look again. Then play.

steps.title: Candidate Moves
steps.text:
Chess is not picking *the* move — it's choosing *between* moves.

Grandmasters look at the board, identify **2–3 candidate moves**, calculate each briefly, and compare. Beginners often calculate one move deeply and play it without ever asking *was there something better?*

## How to generate good candidates

- Start with **forcing moves**: checks, captures, and direct threats.
- Add **developing moves**: bring an idle piece into the game.
- Add **prophylaxis**: stop your opponent's plan before it starts.
- Reject quickly: if a move loses material or violates a principle for no reason, drop it from your shortlist.

Then compare your finalists head-to-head: *which leaves my position the strongest after my opponent's best reply?*

## Why this matters

- It prevents tunnel vision (locking in on the first move you saw).
- It surfaces tactics — sometimes Move B is great precisely because Move A is good.
- It makes you a calmer player, because you've ruled out alternatives instead of guessing.

> Pick three, choose one. Repeat for thirty moves. That's chess.
